deficits.missing: The 'when to use the library vs write it yourself' objective is handled well inside individual chapters (TRL 'Strengths and Limits', vLLM 'not the only serving engine', xformers 'worth reaching for in two cases', llama.cpp 'not the right tool for production throughput'), but there is no single consolidated cross-layer table mapping each concept -> canonical library -> when to build your own -> source-code entry point.
deficits.fix: Add an appendix section (or extend 99-appendix/04-tooling-setup) with a per-layer decision matrix: layer/concept, the canonical OSS library, the specific source file/class to read (e.g. megatron/core/parallel_state.py, vLLM KVCacheManager), and a one-line 'build-it-yourself vs use-the-lib' verdict, cross-linking the deep chapters.

deficits.missing: xformers appears only once (the memory_efficient_attention / SDPA EFFICIENT_ATTENTION fallback note). It is correct and well-placed, but xformers' broader role (diffusion attention, sm<80 coverage, its op registry) is thin relative to the other named libraries.
deficits.fix: Add one short paragraph pointing at xformers' source layout (xformers/ops/fmha) and enumerating the concrete cases where an engineer imports xformers directly vs relies on torch SDPA dispatch.

summary: This cross-cutting 'open-source library coverage' capability is one of the book's strongest areas. Every library on the required list has substantive, internals-level treatment: PyTorch autograd internals (1.7 with custom autograd.Function + gradcheck), Triton (4.4 implements FA fwd+bwd with autograd wiring and correctness tests), FlashAttention/xformers (4.2/4.3), Megatron-LM + DeepSpeed + FSDP (3.5/3.7 with real parallel_state code, ZeRO configs, MFU/HFU accounting, SLURM launch scripts), quantization/bitsandbytes/GGUF-llama.cpp (4.8), TRL (6.3, reference-grade per-trainer walkthrough), veRL (6.4), vLLM (7.3, reference-grade block-manager/scheduler/executor internals), SGLang (7.4), TensorRT-LLM/TGI/llama.cpp/Ollama (7.5), and lm-eval-harness (11.3 with LM abstract-class methods and acc vs acc_norm mechanics). Chapters consistently deliver the three demanded objectives: (1) the real library for each concept, (2) how it implements the concept internally with named classes/files, and (3) when to reach for it vs alternatives vs writing your own. They also include the level-4 markers: source pointers (GitHub links plus specific files like megatron/core/parallel_state.py and vLLM's KVCacheManager/EngineCore/ModelRunner), resource accounting (KV-per-token math, memory footprints, MFU), and failure modes (preemption storms, reward hacking, KL spikes). The deliverable -- 'a reader knows which library to reach for and how it works internally' -- is fully met. Only minor gaps: no single consolidated cross-layer decision matrix, and xformers is comparatively thin.
